source_intake_record.raw_question_text: The single-particle energies for neutrons and protons in the vicinity of ${}_{82}^{208}\text{Pb}_{126}$ are given in Fig. 2.13. Using this figure as a guide, estimate or evaluate the following. The spins and parities of the lowest states of ${}_{83}^{208}\text{Bi}$ (nearly degenerate). What is the energy of the ground state of ${}^{208}\text{Bi}$ relative to ${}^{208}\text{Pb}$?

multi_step: The task requires identifying relevant neutron and proton single-particle states near 208Pb and using them to infer the lowest states of 208Bi.
The wording asks for more than one target output: the nearly degenerate spins/parities and the ground-state energy relative to 208Pb.
This is not a simple lookup; it involves combining information from the figure into a nuclear-structure inference.
verifiability: answer_type=set and answer_present=true make the listed spin-parity outputs structurally easy to compare against a reference.
registry_passed=true and the image readability_score is high, so downstream checking against the figure is feasible.

candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: answer field appears to cover only the spin-parity part of the question, not the ground-state energy part
image field contains a PIL image object string representation rather than a file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain:
For ${ }_{83}^{208}\text{Bi}$, the ground state has an unpaired proton and an unpaired neutron, the proton being in $h_{9/2}$, the neutron being in $p_{1/2}$. As $J=1/2+9/2=5$ (since both nucleon spins are antiparallel to $l$), $l_{p}=5, l_{n}=1$ and so the parity is $(-1)^{l_{p}+l_{n}}=+$, the states has $J^{p}=5^{+}$. The first excited state is formed by a neutron in $f_{5/2}$ transiting to $p_{1/2}$ and its spinparity is determined by the unpaired $f_{5/2}$ neutron and $h_{9/2}$ proton. Hence $J=5/2+9/2=7$, parity is $(-1)^{1+5}=+$, and so $J^{p}=7^{+}$. Therefore, the two lowest states have spin-parity $5^{+}$ and $7^{+}$.

The energy difference between the ground states of ${ }^{208}\text{Bi}$ and ${ }^{208}\text{Pb}$ can be obtained roughly from Fig. 2.13. As compared with ${ }^{208}\text{Pb}$, ${ }^{208}\text{Bi}$ has one more proton at $h_{9/2}$ and one less neutron at $p_{1/2}$ we have
$\
